events.text: Patient is a 61-year-old Black female with a 35 pack-year smoking history (former smoker, quit 10 years ago), ECOG performance status 0, BMI 27.8.

At age 61, patient was diagnosed with non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma (histology 8140), primary site left upper lobe (C34.1), clinical stage IIIA, T2bN2M0, with ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

At age 61, patient was incidentally found to have a left upper lobe nodule on CT angiography performed for evaluation of chest pain in the emergency department. She was otherwise asymptomatic from an oncologic standpoint.

At age 61, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed a 4.1 x 3.6 cm part-solid mass in the left upper lobe with a solid component measuring 3.2 cm. Left aortopulmonary window lymph node enlarged to 1.9 cm and left prevascular lymph node 1.4 cm. No distant metastases.
At age 61, PET/CT showed FDG-avid left upper lobe mass (SUVmax 11.4), left aortopulmonary window lymph node (SUVmax 7.6), and left prevascular lymph node (SUVmax 5.1). No distant metastatic uptake.
At age 61, MRI brain with and without contrast was negative for intracranial metastatic disease.

At age 61, EBUS-guided transbronchial needle aspiration of station 5 (aortopulmonary) and station 6 (prevascular) lymph nodes. Both stations positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma. Concurrent bronchoscopic biopsy of left upper lobe mass confirmed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with solid and acinar patterns. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive, Napsin-A positive, p40 negative. PD-L1 TPS 20% (22C3 assay).

At age 61, next-generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) of tumor specimen: EGFR wild-type, ALK rearrangement not detected, ROS1 rearrangement not detected, BRAF V600E not detected, KRAS G12C detected, STK11 wild-type, TP53 G245S detected. No actionable driver mutations per current guidelines (KRAS G12C inhibitors not approved in first-line setting). Tumor mutational burden 8.4 mut/Mb. Microsatellite stable.

At age 61, multidisciplinary tumor board: unresectable stage IIIA (N2 multistation) adenocarcinoma. Recommended definitive concurrent chemoradiation with carboplatin/pemetrexed followed by durvalumab consolidation. Good performance status and pulmonary function support this approach.
At age 61, initiated concurrent chemoradiation with carboplatin AUC 2 weekly and pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 every 3 weeks during radiation.
At age 61, received definitive thoracic radiation therapy to left upper lobe primary and mediastinal lymph nodes, 60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks.
At age 61, during concurrent chemoradiation week 4, patient developed grade 2 esophagitis with dysphagia to solids. Managed with liquid diet and viscous lidocaine.
At age 61, at completion of chemoradiation, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 esophagitis (improving), and grade 1 radiation dermatitis overlying left chest.

At age 61, restaging CT 4 weeks after completion of chemoradiation showed partial response. Left upper lobe mass decreased to 2.6 x 2.2 cm. Lymphadenopathy decreased. Radiation-related ground-glass changes in left upper lobe. No new lesions.
At age 61, initiated durvalumab 10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks as consolidation immunotherapy following concurrent chemoradiation, to be given for up to 12 months.

At age 62, after 4 months of durvalumab, patient developed grade 1 cough with mild dyspnea. CT showed bilateral ground-glass opacities not present on prior imaging. Suspected grade 1 to 2 radiation recall pneumonitis versus immune-related pneumonitis.

At age 62, durvalumab held for suspected pneumonitis. PFTs showed mild decrease in DLCO. Started prednisone 0.5 mg/kg daily. Symptoms and imaging improved at 2-week follow-up. Prednisone tapered over 4 weeks.
At age 62, resumed durvalumab after pneumonitis resolution. Completed full 12 months of durvalumab consolidation.
At age 62, surveillance CT after completing durvalumab showed continued response. Left upper lobe mass now 1.8 x 1.5 cm, likely mostly fibrosis. No evidence of recurrence or new disease.
At age 62, surveillance CT 6 months post-durvalumab showed stable fibrotic changes in left upper lobe. No evidence of recurrence.

At age 62, no evidence of disease recurrence after completing definitive chemoradiation and durvalumab consolidation. Will continue surveillance with CT every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months.

CLINICAL PROGRESS NOTE

At age 61, patient was incidentally found to have a left upper lobe nodule on CT angiography performed for evaluation of chest pain in the emergency department. She was otherwise asymptomatic from an oncologic standpoint.

The patient was seen in clinic today for ongoing oncologic management.

scenario_blurb: Stage IIIA-IV non-small cell lung cancer without actionable driver mutations (EGFR wild-type, ALK negative, ROS1 negative). Treated with carboplatin/pemetrexed plus pembrolizumab immunotherapy. Mix of adenocarcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma. Some with high PD-L1 expression receiving pembrolizumab monotherapy.
